context:
This course explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn to make strategic decisions related to supply chain network design, supply chain finance, supplier management and procurement, production and material requirements planning, distribution, Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP), and process and organizational design. By the end of the course you will be equipped to optimize supply chain networks, apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions, and lead cross-functional initiatives that balance operational excellence with strategic business objectives. This course is part of the MITx MicroMasters¬Æ Program in Supply Chain Management.

Syllabus:
CTL.SC2x Supply Chain Design

Program:
Part of the MITx MicroMasters Program in Supply Chain Management.

Course Description:
Supply Chain Design: SC2x explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn how to make strategic decisions related to network design, supply chain finance, supplier management, production planning, distribution, and organizational design.

Learning Objectives:
- Design and optimize supply chain networks
- Apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions
- Develop supplier management and procurement strategies
- Plan production and material requirements
- Coordinate Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP) processes
- Improve cross-functional communication and organizational performance

Prerequisites:
There are no formal prerequisites. However, because SC2x is a quantitative course, learners benefit from familiarity with algebra, spreadsheets, and introductory concepts in statistics, probability, and optimization. Learners seeking additional preparation may wish to complete Supply Chain Analytics: SC0x and Supply Chain Fundamentals: SC1x first.

Estimated Effort:
8‚Äì12 hours per week for 12 weeks.

Intended Audience:
Professionals and lifelong learners interested in supply chain management, operations, logistics, and business analytics.

Course Schedule:
New material for each week is released on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC. Graded assignments are due on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC on the specified deadline.
- Week 0 ‚Äî Course Overview & Logistics (starts Jun 17, 2026)
- Week 1 ‚Äî Basic Supply Chain Network Design (Jun 24 ‚Äì Jul 8, 2026)
- Week 2 ‚Äî Advanced Supply Chain Network Design (Jul 1 ‚Äì Jul 15, 2026)
- Week 3 ‚Äî Production Planning (Jul 8 ‚Äì Jul 22, 2026)
- Week 4 ‚Äî Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies (Jul 15 ‚Äì Jul 29, 2026)
- Week 5 ‚Äî Prep Week (Jul 22, 2026) ‚Äî no graded assignment; review for the Midterm
- Week 6 ‚Äî Midterm Exam (Jul 29 ‚Äì Jul 31, 2026)
- Week 7 ‚Äî Supplier Management (Aug 5 ‚Äì Aug 19, 2026)
- Week 8 ‚Äî Supply Chain Finance I (Aug 12 ‚Äì Aug 26, 2026)
- Week 9 ‚Äî Supply Chain Finance II (Aug 19 ‚Äì Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 10 ‚Äî Supply Chain Finance III and Organizational Design (Aug 26 ‚Äì Sep 9, 2026)
- Week 11 ‚Äî Prep Week (Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 12 ‚Äî Final Exam (Sep 9 ‚Äì Sep 11, 2026)

Key Dates:
Course starts Jun 17, 2026; Financial assistance application deadline Jul 8, 2026; Enrollment / Verification upgrade deadline (Certificate Track) Jul 22, 2026; Course ends Sep 11, 2026; Certificates available Sep 18, 2026.

Weekly Topics:
- Week 1 ‚Äî Basic Supply Chain Network Design: Introduces supply chain design and reviews key concepts from SC1x. Formulate and solve network flow models using spreadsheets, then explore the Facility Location Problem, including its formulation and solution as a Mixed Integer Linear Program (MILP).
- Week 2 ‚Äî Advanced Supply Chain Network Design: Combine the Facility Location problem with network flow models to formulate and solve larger network design problems including facility capacity, level of service, and inbound/outbound transportation. Cover modeling multiple products, echelons, and periods; discuss flexibility and robustness.
- Week 3 ‚Äî Production Planning: Move from network design to working with internal and external manufacturing partners. Present the Fixed Planning Horizon model; introduce Bill of Materials (BOM), Material Requirements Planning (MRP), and Distribution Requirements Planning (DRP) systems.
- Week 4 ‚Äî Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies: The S&OP process is presented and discussed, along with different distribution strategies and how e-commerce has affected the design of distribution networks.
- Week 5 ‚Äî Prep Week: Catch up and review; addresses practical concerns of running a network design project, in preparation for the Midterm.
- Week 6 ‚Äî Midterm Exam: Assesses understanding of concepts and methods covered in Weeks 1‚Äì4.
- Weeks 7‚Äì10 ‚Äî Supplier Management and Supply Chain Finance: Procurement strategy and optimization-based procurement; accounting fundamentals and cost accounting; cash flows and discounted cash flows; supply chain finance, financial performance, and organizational/metric design.
- Week 12 ‚Äî Final Exam.

Assessment & Grading:
The course separates a learning space (videos, quick questions, and practice problems with immediate feedback; weekly Graded Assignments as formative assessments, with solutions released after the due date) from an assessment space (the Midterm and Final Exams, which evaluate individual mastery; no solutions or individualized feedback are provided for exam questions). To pass the course and earn a Certificate, you must achieve a final course grade of at least 60%. Grade weights:
- Weekly Graded Assignments: 20%
- Midterm Exam: 35%
- Final Exam: 45%

Enrollment Track Access:
Graded Assignments for Weeks 1, 2, and 3 are available to both Audit and Certificate Track learners. Graded Assignments for Weeks 4, 7, 8, 9, and 10, the Midterm Exam, and the Final Exam are available only to Certificate Track learners.

Graded Assignments are released with each content week and may be completed any time before the deadline (deadlines are firm; no extensions). Point values are indicated per question; most questions allow two submission attempts; only the last submission is graded; you must click Submit for each answer. A green/red indicator shows correctness after submission, and official solutions and explanations are released after the deadline. Assignments remain available for review afterward.

Exams: the Midterm and Final together account for 80% of the final grade. They are open-book (you may consult course videos, slides, Key Concept Documents, and your own notes) and timed. Each exam is available during a 48-hour testing window; once started you have 2.5 hours, and the timer cannot be paused, stopped, or reset (start at least 2.5 hours before the deadline for full time). The Discussion Forum and Progress Tab are disabled while an exam is open. Unlike Graded Assignments, exams provide no correctness feedback and no solutions after the deadline. Timed exams must be completed in a web browser.

Policies & Support:
Academic integrity: All graded work must comply with MIT Learn's Honor Code and Academic Integrity Policy ‚Äî original work; no unauthorized collaboration; no use of generative AI tools unless explicitly permitted by the teaching team; a single account; fair competition; and protecting assessment integrity (do not post, share, or distribute answers). Violations may result in invalidation of graded work, certificate revocation, or removal from the course.
Communication: The Discussion Forum (monitored by Community Teaching Assistants and staff) is for course concepts, videos, quick questions, practice problems, and general problem-solving approaches; do not use it for Weekly Graded Assignments, the Midterm, or the Final. Assessment-related questions go to the email help desk at sc2xhelp@mit.edu (include the week, problem number, and a description); staff will not review calculations, verify answers, or provide hints for graded work.
Accessibility: For disability-related accommodations, contact mitx-accessibility@mit.edu as early as possible (at least two weeks before an exam opens for exam accommodations).

Course Team:
Instructors: Dr. Chris Caplice; Prof. Yossi Sheffi; Dr. Jarrod Goentzel; Mr. Jim Rice. Course Lead: Ms. Laura Allegue. Teaching Assistant: Mr. Matt Toy. MicroMasters in SCM Team: Dr. Eva Ponce (Director); Dr. Inma Borrella (Academic Lead); Ms. Cara Greaney; Mr. Kellen Betts; Mr. Paulo de Sousa; Mr. Jeff Baker; Mr. Robert Cummings; Mr. Connor Makowski; Ms. Elise Yonika. Course help / assessment questions: sc2xhelp@mit.edu

Exercise:
TITLE: Week 2 Graded Assignment ‚Äî Advanced Supply Chain Network Design

This assignment extends the network design models with capacities, fixed costs, service constraints, and multi-commodity flows. Build and re-solve spreadsheet/Python optimization models for each part.

Graded Assignment 1: Extra Scenarios

At the end of the "Advanced Scenarios" video, Dr. Caplice asked you to create new scenarios and play with the spreadsheet. This graded assignment focuses on doing exactly that. You will create a series of six new scenarios. Each new scenario builds upon the previous one, so get each one right before moving on. Sanity checks along the way are ungraded.

Part 0 (ungraded): Scenario 10 is the starting point for the first new scenario. Reproduce Scenario 10 exactly as described in the video. What is the total cost of Scenario 10? (Reproduce the result with the spreadsheet; do not copy the answer from the video. Answer in dollars, no dollar sign or comma.)

Part 1 (ungraded sanity check): Create Scenario 11 ‚Äî identical to Scenario 10 except the BF Plant is assigned a capacity of 2000 units. Solve optimally. How many units are shipped from the BF Plant to the BO DC? (Zero / 450 / 480 / 500)

Part 2: What is the optimal cost for Scenario 11?

Part 3: Create Scenario 12 ‚Äî identical to Scenario 11, but for the first time assign a fixed cost to operating each plant (paid only if the plant is used): $14,700 for plant BFP and $14,700 for plant SCP. Solve optimally. What is the optimal cost for Scenario 12?

Part 4: Create Scenario 13 ‚Äî identical to Scenario 12, except the fixed cost of the SC Plant is reduced to $10,700 (BFP remains $14,700). Solve optimally. What is the optimal cost for Scenario 13?

Part 5 (ungraded sanity check): Create Scenario 14 ‚Äî identical to Scenario 13, except now allow a more relaxed maximum weighted average distance of customers to DCs of 35 miles (instead of 25) and a more lenient minimum percentage of customers within 50 miles of DCs of 60% (instead of 80%). Solve optimally. Which DCs did we stop using, compared to the previous scenario? (We still use all the DCs / We stopped using only the PR DC / We stopped using only the BO DC / We stopped using both BO and PR)

Part 6: What is the optimal cost for Scenario 14?

Part 7: Create Scenario 15 ‚Äî identical to Scenario 14, except the BO DC has become more efficient with a new variable cost of $1.00 per unit and a new fixed cost of $5,000. Solve optimally. What is the optimal cost for Scenario 15?

Part 8 (ungraded sanity check): Create Scenario 16 ‚Äî identical to Scenario 15, except there is a new consumer, Ferrisbourgh, with demand of 120 units and distances to the DCs (miles): 70 to BO, 65 to NA, 85 to PR, 50 to SP, 70 to WO. Solve optimally. Which is closer to the new cost? (82700 / 83100 / 83500 / 83900)

Part 9: What is the optimal cost for Scenario 16?

Graded Assignment 2: CMS Manufacturer

CMS Inc. is a large manufacturer of industrial parts and components based in Chicago, IL. The company distributes through a network of three warehouses in Atlanta, GA; Charlotte, NC; and Jacksonville, FL. Its customer base includes original equipment manufacturers across the Southeastern U.S. Monthly demand aggregated by state:

State   Demand (cases)
AL      490
FL      1880
GA      1060
KY      467
NC      1049
SC      515
TN      685
VA      854

Each warehouse can handle a limited number of cases per month:
- Atlanta, GA: 3500 cases
- Charlotte, NC: 3500 cases
- Jacksonville, FL: 3500 cases

It costs 1.9 $/mile to ship one case from the manufacturing plant to the warehouses. Distances (miles) from the Chicago, IL plant to the three warehouses:
              Atlanta, GA   Charlotte, NC   Jacksonville, FL
Chicago, IL      717            756              1100

It costs 4.9 $/mile to move one case from the warehouses to the customers' states. Distances (miles) between warehouses and states:
                  AL    FL    GA    KY    NC    SC    TN    VA
Atlanta, GA      189   509     5   490   304   351   280   581
Charlotte, NC    458   524   276   440     5   217   481   310
Jacksonville,FL  372   141   368   746   420   238   617   656

According to a distribution network optimization conducted 5 years ago: all demand from AL, GA, KY, SC, and TN ships from Atlanta, GA; Charlotte, NC serves NC and VA; Jacksonville, FL serves only FL.

Part 1: Based on the current customer allocation, what is the actual transportation cost (inbound + outbound)? (Round to nearest integer.)

Part 2: The company updates the customer allocation by running a new network optimization model to minimize transportation cost. What is the optimal transportation cost (inbound + outbound)? (Round to nearest integer.)

Part 3: CMS also wants to revisit the number of warehouses open, including a monthly fixed cost for running each warehouse: Atlanta $2,000,000; Charlotte $2,000,000; Jacksonville $2,000,000. What is the optimal total cost (fixed + transportation)? (Round to nearest integer.)

Part 4: Based on the optimized model from Part 3, how many warehouses need to remain open?

Part 5: From your Part 3 answer, what percentage of demand is fulfilled within 350 miles (warehouse-to-state distance ‚â§ 350 miles)? (Percentage with one decimal place, e.g., 89.5.)

Graded Assignment 3: WUWU Expanded

This assignment revisits the WUWU Problem from this week's Lesson 2 (Video 3). Download the WUWU spreadsheet (available for Excel and LibreOffice). Use the WUWU 1 problem (WUWU1 tab, not WUWU2). As it stands, the objective function for WUWU1 has an optimal value of $6,525.00 ‚Äî confirm you get the same value before starting. The parts below modify the model's parameters and structure. Give all cost answers in dollars, numbers only, rounded to the nearest integer.

Part 1: Change the capacity in both DCs ‚Äî increase the Atlanta DC from 200 to 300 widgets, and decrease the Boston DC from 500 to 400 widgets. Solve optimally. What is the new minimum cost?

Part 2: Building on Part 1, add a constraint: the Miami plant now has an overall capacity of 250 widgets of any type. Calculate the new minimum cost and compare to Part 1. Did the new constraint make any difference in the solution? (Consider why.)

Part 3: Building on Part 2, add a constraint: the Chicago plant now has an overall capacity of 250 widgets of any type. Solve optimally. What is the new minimum cost?

Part 4: Building on Part 3, add two constraints: a 150-widget capacity at the Atlanta DC specific to widget 1 (Atlanta processes at most 150 units of widget 1), and a 200-unit constraint at the Boston DC specific to widget 2. Solve optimally. What is the new minimum cost?

Part 5: Building on Part 4, add a new commodity, Widget 4.
Demand for Widget 4: 20 units in NY, 20 units in VA, 10 units in PA.
Production capacity for Widget 4: up to 20 in Chicago, up to 40 in Dallas, up to 25 in Miami.
Transportation costs for Widget 4, plants to DCs: Chicago‚ÜíAtlanta 6, Chicago‚ÜíBoston 5, Dallas‚ÜíAtlanta 4, Dallas‚ÜíBoston 7, Miami‚ÜíAtlanta 5, Miami‚ÜíBoston 8.
Transportation costs for Widget 4, DCs to regions: Atlanta‚ÜíNY 8, Atlanta‚ÜíVA 6, Atlanta‚ÜíPA 6, Boston‚ÜíNY 5, Boston‚ÜíVA 6, Boston‚ÜíPA 5.
What is the new minimum cost of the optimal solution?

[local:lecture_01_20_video_3_solving_part_3]
PROFESSOR: Welcome to part 3 in this recitation of optimization using spreadsheets. We will start adding additional complexity as we go through the different parts of this problem. And now it's time to add a new constraint, which is related to the capacity of the assembly plants. If you haven't yet, please pause this video. Go through the problem statement. Try to populate your spreadsheet with the new numbers, and return to us in a few minutes. Hopefully, you are all set. So let's go through it together. Problem statement is telling us that there has been a disruption in spare part availability that affected capacity of our assembly plants. So let's analyze what are the elements that now we have in our problem? Please also double check that the profit maximization approach has been disregarded by the team analyzing these results. So this is what we talk about in terms of not overriding your model as you go through different parts of a problem but actually having it in different tabs of your spreadsheet. So as of now, if I need to replicate one of the models, the one closer to what I need is the one in part 1, not the one in part 2. So this is why we suggest you to do every part of every problem in different tabs. And I promise, it's not adding extra effort or extra time. It will save you a lot of time. Question one, as you know, is going to ask about the elements. So we're using the model in question 1. So just let's remember what we had there. We still had the objective function, the demand constraint, and the non-negativity constraint. We're still not needing binary variables, linking constraint, or conservation of flow. But we are actually needing now a supply constraint because we have a capacity one. So let's click it, submit, and see how it goes. Great. So remember, using the Show Answer button if you want to see the model formulation. And join me using the spreadsheet to find the minimum transportation cost with this new piece of information regarding capacity constraint. So we read the problem. We understood the model. We know now the elements we need. We're going to replicate part 1 model, as we talked. I'm just moving to the end, same book, creating a copy, and renaming it to part 3. And I'm ready to bring the new numbers provided in the problem statement.

[local:lecture_00_04_key_concept_document]
The capacity is the maximum throughput over
a speciÔ¨Åed unit of time (e.g. a week). Note that this can be very diÔ¨Écult to measure in practice, since
shifts can be added, while capacity also depends on planning and scheduling. Network Design Baselines
You should be careful in which baseline you use for comparing your results. Three baselines are important
to consider:
‚Ä¢ Baseline 1 ‚Äì Actual costs: what cost does the model give us if we use the actual, current Ô¨Çows? We
want to know how well the model matches reality. You use this baseline to calibrate the model. ‚Ä¢ Baseline 2 ‚Äì Adhere to policy: there may be a policy in place that you are simply not adhering to in
your operations. With this baseline you want to know how other solutions compare to what you ought
to be doing according to the policy in place. ‚Ä¢ Baseline 3 ‚Äì Optimal DC assignment: if you were to use the optimal assignment for the DC ‚Äì what
would the solution look like? With this baseline you can isolate the eÔ¨Äect of number of DCs since you
optimize allocation. It is important to keep in mind that you compare design changes to the right baseline. For instance,
if you use the model to Ô¨Ågure out how much you could reduce costs by reducing the number of DCs, you
need to be aware the model will tell you this while, at the same time, optimizing the allocation given the
number of DCs. Hence you should compare your solution - the ‚Äúoptimal number of DCs under optimal
allocation‚Äù - to Baseline 3, where you have the ‚Äúcurrent number of DCs under optimal allocation‚Äù. Why? Because otherwise you compare solutions that have optimal allocations to solutions that do not have
optimal allocations ‚Äì you do not isolate the eÔ¨Äect of changing the number of DCs. Running Scenarios
One of the key beneÔ¨Åts of the Supply Chain Network Design Model is that it is easy and fast to run
diÔ¨Äerent scenarios. Scenarios can investigate uncertain parameters (sensitivity analysis) or explore how
diÔ¨Äerent constraints aÔ¨Äect the optimal solution. While this is a great beneÔ¨Åt, it is important not to get ‚Äúanalysis paralysis‚Äù ‚Äì just because it is possible
to run many scenarios does not mean it is the way to go. It is important to understand which scenarios
are relevant and, most importantly, how to interpret the results. For instance, which baseline should be
used for comparing the results of the scenario?

[local:lecture_02_05_key_take_aways]
All right. Another pretty intensive lesson here. So hopefully, you're getting a handle on things though, because we only did one thing during this lesson. We used this example of the New England Root Beer Distributors to show how to use network design models to test the different design scenarios. Previous lessons showed us how to build the models, and we played with them, and showed how the optimization worked. Now we apply them. And I tried to show through a very simple example, the three major steps. The first step, collecting the data, which is the most critical step, and the most commonly ignored step. It's the one that people gloss over because it's very hard. But you need to collect data that is representative and makes sense. The data has to be costs and capacities on the transportation arcs and the facilities. We'll talk much more about this in next week's lessons but this is always the hardest part and most time consuming part of any network design. The second thing we did, we built baselines. Why did we build a baseline? We need to make sure that our model closely resembles reality. That's why we use existing flows. A model that doesn't reflect what happens today is useless. No one will believe it in the future. So you have to make sure that the mechanics of the model and the costs of the model represent reality. And it's a great way to test your data. To make sure that the individual components of the data make sense. Make sure that the total costs look reasonable, but also make sure that the different components, the facility cost, the distribution cost, the transportation costs, make sure those buckets are also somewhat reasonable. Then we use the baseline. We optimized that to be able to compare it to the new designs. You don't want to just compare last year's bad execution to a future plan with perfect execution. You have to make it apples to apples, and that's why we created the other baselines. Because we said OK, now let's take away the poor execution because when I do future models, it's going to assume that it's executed perfectly, because we're all perfect in the future. So we need to make sure I'm comparing apples to apples and not apples to my baseline one, which is more like a rotten apple. It has all that bad execution in it. All right. The last thing we did was the fun part, and that's running the scenarios.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I‚Äôm working on the Week 2 supply chain design assignment rn, and I‚Äôm kinda stuck on setting up the optimization for the CMS manufacturer part. can u help me figure out how to structure Part 1 vs Part 2 without just jumping to the final numbers?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you think through it! Before we dive in ‚Äî what's your understanding of the key difference between Part 1 and Part 2? What do you think each one is actually asking you to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help structuring Parts 1 and 2 of the CMS assignment. They haven't shown any work yet, so I should start with a Socratic question to assess where they actually are and what specifically is confusing them, rather than launching into explanation. I want to understand if they're confused about the model structure, the data setup, or the difference between the two parts. Keeping it bite-sized and letting them lead with their current thinking.

exchanges.student: I think Part 1 uses the old warehouse-to-state assignment as fixed, and Part 2 re-optimizes the allocations? ngl I‚Äôm not fully sure if inbound from Chicago also changes in Part 1 or if only outbound is fixed.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on the core distinction! Now think about this: in Part 1, once the warehouse-to-state assignments are fixed, what determines how much inventory each warehouse needs to receive from Chicago? Does Chicago have any choice in how much to send where?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good grasp of the Part 1 vs Part 2 distinction. Their uncertainty is specifically about whether inbound flows (Chicago to warehouses) are fixed in Part 1 or only outbound flows. This is a good conceptual question. I should ask them to think about what 'current customer allocation' means in the problem statement, and whether the inbound flows are determined by the outbound flows. This will help them reason it out without me giving the answer.

exchanges.student: I think Chicago doesn‚Äôt really have a choice there, bc each warehouse has to receive enough to cover the states assigned to it. so inbound to each warehouse should just equal total demand of the states it serves, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So given that, can you lay out the two components of the total cost formula you'll need to calculate for Part 1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned that inbound flows are determined by the fixed outbound allocations. This is exactly right. I should affirm this and then nudge them toward actually computing the cost, asking them to think about the formula they need to apply.

exchanges.student: yeah I think it‚Äôs inbound cost from Chicago to each warehouse plus outbound cost from each warehouse to its assigned states. I‚Äôm just a little fuzzy on whether I should multiply demand by distance first and then by the per-mile case cost, or if there‚Äôs any extra term.
exchanges.tutor: You've got it ‚Äî cost = demand √ó distance √ó cost per mile. No extra terms. Now go ahead and calculate Part 1 with the fixed assignments given in the problem. What number do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right structure. Their question is about the formula mechanics - demand √ó distance √ó cost per mile. This is straightforward and they basically have it right. I should confirm their formula thinking with a quick guiding question to make sure they apply it correctly, then encourage them to go calculate.

exchanges.student: I haven‚Äôt computed it yet tbh ‚Äî can u sanity check my warehouse demand totals first? I got ATL = 3217, Charlotte = 1903, Jacksonville = 1880.
exchanges.tutor: Those totals are correct. Now you have everything you need ‚Äî go ahead and compute the full Part 1 cost!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants me to verify their warehouse demand totals before computing. I should check these against the assignment data. ATL serves AL, GA, KY, SC, TN: 490+1060+467+515+685 = 3217. Charlotte serves NC and VA: 1049+854 = 1903. Jacksonville serves FL: 1880. All three are correct. I can confirm this and push them to move forward with the cost calculation.

exchanges.student: ok nice, thanks ‚Äî I got 8,922,841, but that feels weirdly specific so I‚Äôm not super confident.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to double-check! Walk me through one piece of your calculation ‚Äî say, the inbound cost for Atlanta. What did you compute there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a number but is uncertain. Rather than confirming or denying it, I should ask them to walk me through their calculation so they can catch any errors themselves. This keeps them engaged in the process and helps identify where a mistake might be if there is one.

exchanges.student: I did 3217 √ó 717 √ó 1.9, which gave 4,379,897.1. idk if I punched that wrong though.
exchanges.tutor: Your approach is right, but your number is slightly off. Try recomputing 3217 √ó 717 √ó 1.9 carefully ‚Äî what do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I can verify this quickly: 3217 √ó 717 = 2,305,589; √ó 1.9 = 4,380,619.1. The student got 4,379,897.1, which is slightly off ‚Äî likely a rounding or keying error. I should point out there's a small discrepancy and ask them to recheck their arithmetic rather than giving the correct number.

exchanges.student: oh wow yeah, I get 4,379,975.1 now. can u give me a small hint on checking the rest fast without redoing literally everything?
exchanges.tutor: Still a small discrepancy there ‚Äî try breaking it into two steps: first compute 3217 √ó 717, then multiply that result by 1.9. As for checking the rest efficiently, what if you set up a small table with one row per warehouse leg? That way each step is visible and easier to catch errors.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's number is still slightly off (correct is 4,380,619.1). I should point this out again gently and encourage them to be careful with the multiplication. For the hint on checking the rest, I can suggest a systematic approach without doing it for them.

exchanges.student: got it, I‚Äôll do that ‚Äî I think I was rushing fr. after I fix Part 1, can I ask u how to set up Part 2 with decision variables and capacity constraints?
exchanges.tutor: Absolutely, but let's get Part 1 locked in first. Share your final number once you've redone the calculations, and then we'll tackle Part 2 setup.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking ahead about Part 2 setup. I should keep them focused on finishing Part 1 correctly first before moving on, consistent with the assignment's own guidance ('get each one right before moving on'). I'll encourage them to finish and share their Part 1 result first.

exchanges.student: yeah fair, I‚Äôll recalc Part 1 first and send it. thanks, this helped.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good ‚Äî take your time with it. Come back with your Part 1 total and we'll go from there!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up and will come back with their Part 1 result. I should give a brief encouraging closing and remind them to return when ready. No need for more scaffolding at this point.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well across all rubric dimensions. The conversation was consistently Socratic: the tutor asked guiding questions rather than providing answers, confirmed correct student reasoning, and redirected the student to recheck their own arithmetic rather than correcting it outright. Scaffolding was well-paced, building from conceptual understanding to formula structure to numerical execution. No submission-ready content was provided, no off-topic drift occurred, and tone was supportive throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready output. Every turn either posed a guiding question or confirmed a student-generated step, keeping reasoning student-led throughout. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student contribution (e.g., confirming demand totals before moving to cost calculation) and used targeted follow-up questions rather than dumping information. 1.3: When the student made an arithmetic error, the tutor gave process-level guidance (break into two steps, use a table) rather than just flagging incorrectness. 2.1‚Äì2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift were observed. 3.1‚Äì3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging, and no grading language was used.
